tech_stack.ecommerce_platform: Salesforce Commerce Cloud + Custom Next.js/GraphQL layer
tech_stack.primary_platform: **Hybrid: Salesforce Commerce Cloud (SFCC) + Custom Proprietary Build**
tech_stack.tech_stack_summary: Nike operates a fully custom, AWS-native DTC ecommerce platform at massive scale ($46B revenue, ~4B monthly visits). Frontend: Next.js + React on Node.js. CDN/WAF: Akamai (primary) + AWS CloudFront. Commerce logic: Salesforce Commerce Cloud (hybrid) + heavy proprietary build. Analytics: Adobe Analytics + Adobe Launch (primary tag manager) + Google Analytics. Search: NETWORK-CONFIRMED proprietary in-house search platform via api.nike.com/search/ (SAYT/suggestions) and product-proxy-v2.adtech-prod.nikecloud.com/products (search results). Nike AI Search (Aug 2025, iOS app + nike.com fragment) also entirely in-house. Personalization: Monetate (A/B + web personalization) + internal CDP. Security/Fraud: Forter + Accertify + iovation. API: GraphQL microservices (GRAND initiative). Cloud: AWS (DynamoDB, Lambda, Step Functions, S3, Neptune). No Algolia, Coveo, Bloomreach, or Constructor detected in live network traffic — confirmed GREENFIELD displacement opportunity targeting Nike's proprietary search stack.

ai_search_gap.app_ai_description: Conversational AI-powered in-app search using 'best-in-class foundational models fine-tuned by domain experts'. Handles natural language queries like 'running shoes for a 5k'. Website search on nike.com appears separate and not powered by NikeAI.
ai_search_gap.narrative: NikeAI is present on nike.com as a conversational UI layer in the SAYT overlay ("Ask NikeAI" with green AI icon). However, the underlying search infrastructure remains Nike Proprietary at api.nike.com/search — a custom Elasticsearch implementation with no third-party search vendor detected. NikeAI is a surface-level AI wrapper; the foundation under it is not AI-native.
ai_search_gap.algolia_angle: You already shipped NikeAI. The search infrastructure powering it — api.nike.com/search, custom Elasticsearch — is the bottleneck. Algolia gives NikeAI a real foundation: sub-20ms responses, AI-native ranking, and merchandising rules without custom engineering.

tech_stack.architecture_notes: Frontend: Next.js + React; Cloud: AWS (Amazon Web Services) — primary
full_list: Ecommerce: Salesforce Commerce Cloud + Custom Next.js/GraphQL layer

search_abandonment.narrative: 9.27% of outbound traffic goes to Google (↑117.81% MoM) — users abandoning on-site search and returning to Google to find products

ai_referral.narrative: ChatGPT is sending 16.55% of referral traffic (↑49.74% MoM). AI chatbots represent 19.30% of all referral traffic — your buyers discover products via AI before visiting your site.

findings.title: Silent Typo Correction — No “Did You Mean” Feedback
findings.expected_behavior: After correcting a typo, display “Did you mean: jordan 4?” so users know their query was interpreted differently.
findings.actual_behavior: Typo silently corrected. Results page shows original misspelling in header (“jrodan 4 (58)”). No correction indicator.
findings.impact_stat: Search visitors convert at 1.8x–3x the rate of non-searchers
findings.prospect_description: User types “jrodan 4” on nike.com, gets Jordan 4 results, but the header reads “jrodan 4 (58)”. No correction confirmation.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia surfaces explicit “Showing results for: jordan 4” correction banner. Zero additional clicks.

findings.title: Location Intent Failure — “Store Near Me” Returns Products
findings.expected_behavior: Route to store locator page or surface Nike Store locations.
findings.actual_behavior: Returns 41 product results (t-shirts, sportswear). Store locator not surfaced.
findings.impact_stat: 80% of shoppers leave a site when search fails to answer their query
findings.prospect_description: A shopper searching “store near me” on nike.com gets t-shirts. The store locator exists (in utility nav) but is invisible to the search engine.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Rules Engine detects location-intent queries and triggers redirect to store locator. Configurable with no engineering deployment.

findings.title: No Federated Search in SAYT — Products-Only Autocomplete
findings.expected_behavior: SAYT shows text suggestions + product thumbnails + category suggestions (e.g., “Running Shoes > Men’s”) + editorial content.
findings.actual_behavior: SAYT shows text suggestions and 5 product thumbnails only. No category or content federation.
findings.impact_stat: 30–50% of ecommerce sessions include a search interaction
findings.prospect_description: Typing “running” shows word suggestions and product cards. No “Running > Trail Shoes” category route. Users must wade through 152 generic results.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Federated Search surfaces products, categories, content, and help articles in a single SAYT panel. Each drawer independently ranked.

findings.title: Static Facets — Context-Blind Filter Panel
findings.expected_behavior: Facets should adapt to product type. Apparel query should show Size, Fit, Color, Material.
findings.actual_behavior: “Cushioning Type” filter appears for a jacket query. Same facet set regardless of product category.
findings.impact_stat: 90% of apparel sites fail ecommerce UX best practices
findings.prospect_description: Search “Dri-FIT UV running jacket” on nike.com and the left rail shows “Cushioning Type” — a shoe attribute — as a filter option for an apparel product.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Dynamic Faceting reads the result set composition and renders only relevant filters. Configurable per category from Algolia Dashboard.

findings.title: Zero Personalization — Browsing History Ignored in Search
findings.expected_behavior: After a user browses running shoes, subsequent broad searches should re-rank to surface running shoes first.
findings.actual_behavior: After browsing running shoes, searching “shoes” shows Jordan 1 Retro High OG first. Zero personalization.
findings.impact_stat: Personalization delivers 50% increase in conversion rate
findings.prospect_description: Browse running shoes on nike.com, search “shoes”. You get Jordan retros. Nike’s search has no memory of the session.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Personalization re-ranks results in real time using user affinity signals. SDK integrates with Nike’s existing Adobe Analytics event stream.

findings.title: NLP Partial — Price and Attribute Filters Not Auto-Applied
findings.tested_query: best shoes for trail running under 150
findings.expected_behavior: Natural language with price constraint should auto-apply max price filter of $150 and surface only trail running footwear.
findings.actual_behavior: “Under 150” NOT enforced. Results include $159 and $160 products. Activity understood but price filter ignored.
findings.impact_stat: AI-powered search delivers 10–30% conversion improvement over keyword matching
findings.prospect_description: A shopper searches “best shoes for trail running under 150” and has to manually scroll past $159 and $160 products. Search heard “trail running” but missed “under 150.”
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia NeuralSearch parses multi-attribute natural language queries and auto-applies corresponding filters as pre-applied filter chips.

findings.title: Competitor Queries Treated as Gibberish — Missed Merchandising Opportunity
findings.expected_behavior: Competitor product query should trigger competitive redirect or surface comparable Nike products.
findings.actual_behavior: Returns identical zero-results page as gibberish query. Same 3 fallback products. No competitive cross-sell logic.
findings.impact_stat: Up to 3.5x conversions from search when recommendations enabled
findings.prospect_description: Someone searches “Yeezy Boost 350” on nike.com — clearly in the sneaker market. Nike returns the exact same zero-results page as a random keyboard smash.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Rules Engine detects competitor brand queries and triggers targeted merchandising: banner with comparable Nike products or curated collection.

buying_committee.name: Venkatesh (Venky) Alagirisamy
buying_committee.title: EVP & Chief Operating Officer
buying_committee.message: Absorbed CTO role December 2025. All technology platform decisions route through him. Frame Algolia as operational capability — fewer Cognizant support tickets, less Elasticsearch maintenance, more engineering velocity.

buying_committee.title: Sr Director, Nike Direct Digital Commerce EMEA
buying_committee.message: 3 NDDC Sr Director seats open following Dec 2025 restructure. Incoming hires will evaluate vendor relationships in their first 30-90 days. Engage before they inherit the status quo.

engagement_phases.label: Immediate — SFCC Co-Sell via CIO Jacob
engagement_phases.message: Contact Algolia's Salesforce Commerce Cloud partner team. Request joint introduction to Jacob via Nike's Salesforce account team. First message frame: 'You built the SFCC ecosystem at Salesforce — Algolia is the search layer purpose-built for it. Nike's next search decision is a one-call conversation, not a six-month evaluation.'

engagement_phases.label: Parallel — Cognizant SI Warm Intro
engagement_phases.target: Cognizant Retail Account Team (Sushant Warikoo) → Nike Engineering
engagement_phases.message: Cognizant holds a 5-year IT services contract with Nike (signed April 2023). Their scope includes nike.com application support — they touch Nike's search infrastructure daily. Pitch: 'Algolia reduces your Elasticsearch support burden at Nike. Fewer custom tickets, faster SLA.'

engagement_phases.label: Economic Buyer — CFO Framing Before First Call
engagement_phases.message: Prepare ROI narrative grounded in Friend's own words: 'Digital is too promotional' → Algolia drives full-price conversion through relevance. Conservative ROI: $96M/year at 1% lift on $9.6B digital baseline. Nike FY ends May 31 — FY2027 budget discussions run September–November 2026.

objection_counters.objection: We built NikeAI ourselves — we can build search too.
objection_counters.counter: NikeAI is the UI surface. api.nike.com/search — the Elasticsearch foundation under it — is the bottleneck. Network-confirmed: zero NLP, no personalization, 'Cushioning Type' filter on jacket queries. Algolia replaces that foundation. You keep NikeAI's interface, you fix the infrastructure.
objection_counters.source: 02-tech-stack.md (NikeAI surface layer vs Elasticsearch foundation confirmed in network traffic)

objection_counters.objection: We're rationalizing tech spend, not adding vendors.
objection_counters.counter: Friend said 'rationalize programs and leverage advanced capabilities' in the same breath. Algolia replaces a Cognizant-supported Elasticsearch cluster with managed SaaS — fewer FTE, fewer support tickets, faster merchandising velocity. This is rationalization, not addition.

objection_counters.objection: We have an internal search team — we don't need a vendor.
objection_counters.counter: Three NDDC Sr Director seats and the Foundational Platform Services seat are simultaneously vacant after the December restructure. The search team has a leadership gap right now. Algolia bridges it while incoming leadership evaluates the stack — and makes the new Sr Director look like they upgraded search on day one.

industry_context.trend_headline: AI-powered search delivers 10–30% conversion lift over keyword matching as sporting goods ecommerce races toward $130B
industry_context.algolia_angle: Nike operates in a sporting goods market where search visitors convert at 1.8x–3x the rate of non-searchers and AI-powered search delivers 10–30% conversion improvement over keyword matching, making Algolia's NLP search, AI personalization, and dynamic re-ranking directly tied to recovering share of Nike's 80.3% apparel cart abandonment rate and closing the search quality gap with Under Armour (+35% conversion on Algolia).

case_studies.result: +35% conversion rate when customers use search
case_studies.why: Under Armour is Nike’s direct competitor in performance athletic footwear and apparel. They moved off a manual, in-house search platform — the same Elasticsearch DIY model Nike uses today — to Algolia and immediately achieved 35% higher search conversion.

case_studies.result: +150% sales contribution from search; +37% conversion rate; +314% search use on mobile; <50ms results
case_studies.product: Algolia Search + Personalization across 11 global online shops
case_studies.why: Lacoste operates 11 international online shops — comparable to Nike’s multi-market, multilingual DTC digital footprint. Lacoste more than doubled global sales contribution from search after deploying Algolia across its global estate.

case_studies.result: +50% conversion rate with personalization; +36% CTR; no-results rate dropped from 5% to 1.8%; 80% mobile traffic
case_studies.why: Decathlon Singapore is the world’s largest sporting goods retailer, with 25,000+ SKUs and 80% mobile traffic — matching Nike’s 79.71% mobile-first profile. The personalization-driven 50% conversion lift maps directly to Nike’s most critical gap.

recommended_first_play.headline: SFCC co-sell via new Nike CIO
recommended_first_play.detail: Nike’s new CIO Cheryan Jacob (hired August 2025) is a former Salesforce executive. Algolia has a purpose-built SFCC AppExchange integration. Trigger the SFCC co-sell motion immediately — Jacob will recognize the Algolia-SFCC value proposition and this creates a direct entry to the commerce/search engineering team without cold outreach.

golden_angle.metric: 35% higher conversion rate when using search
golden_angle.verbatim_quote: Delivering a fast, relevant experience to our online customers is a top priority. With Algolia, Under Armour has been able to move from a manual, time-intensive search tool to a fast, intuitive and insight-driven search experience to deliver what our customers are searching for.
golden_angle.quote_attribution: Nick Maupin, Product Manager, Under Armour
golden_angle.talk_track: Under Armour — your direct competitor — is already on Algolia and achieved 35% higher conversion rate when using search. Nike runs Elasticsearch (internal/DIY). The benchmark exists. The business case is proven.

strategic_angles.label: The NikeAI Gap: App Has AI Search, Website Does Not
strategic_angles.hook: You already built the vision with NikeAI in the app. Algolia is how you extend that same intent-based search experience to nike.com in 90 days, without a custom build.
pain_points: Nike launched NikeAI in August 2025 — conversational AI assistant for iOS Nike App only
Nike.com website search runs on self-managed Elasticsearch — no hosted AI search vendor detected
NikeAI proves internal buy-in for intent-based search; website is the gap
strategic_angles.discovery_question: NikeAI proves your organisation already decided intent-based search is the right direction — what is the roadmap for bringing that same experience to nike.com, and who owns that decision?
strategic_angles.algolia_proof: Algolia NeuralSearch delivers the same intent-based discovery on the web without a custom model build.
strategic_angles.objection: We have NikeAI — we're already doing AI search.
strategic_angles.objection_counter: NikeAI is iOS app-only. nike.com website runs Elasticsearch. The majority of digital revenue is generated on the website, not the app. Algolia closes the web gap NikeAI left open.
strategic_angles.source: [FACT — 09b-social-signals.md (Signal 1 — NikeAI launch, July 2025); 02-tech-stack.md (Elasticsearch DIY, no search vendor detected)]

strategic_angles.label: The Competitor Proof Point: Under Armour Is Already Winning with Algolia
strategic_angles.hook: Under Armour moved off a proprietary search stack and gained 35% more conversions from search. JD Sports, which carries your product in 38 countries, just went live on Algolia. Nike is the only major player still on DIY Elasticsearch.
pain_points: Under Armour (Nike's #3 direct competitor) — confirmed Algolia customer with +35% conversion on search
JD Sports — channel partner in 3,400+ stores in 38 territories — selected Algolia as AI search platform
Nike runs identical proprietary Elasticsearch infrastructure that Under Armour replaced
strategic_angles.discovery_question: Under Armour replaced the same in-house search stack you're running and got a 35% conversion lift — that case study is public. When you benchmark your search conversion against peers, what does that number look like today?
strategic_angles.algolia_proof: Under Armour (direct competitor): +35% higher conversion rate on search, replacing in-house open-source platform. [FACT — algolia.com/customers/under-armour, WebFetch 2026-04-14]
strategic_angles.objection: We built our own search infrastructure — it's part of our data/AI stack.
strategic_angles.objection_counter: Nike built Datalogue, Zodiac, and Celect for data intelligence. But Under Armour also ran a custom search stack, migrated to Algolia, and got 35% more conversions. Build vs. buy for search infrastructure is a resolved question in your vertical.
strategic_angles.source: [FACT — 04-competitors.md (Under Armour Algolia case study confirmed, algolia.com/customers/under-armour WebFetch 2026-04-14); partner-intel.md (JD Sports)]

strategic_angles.label: The Revenue Recovery Math: Search Is the Conversion Lever Nike Needs Right Now
strategic_angles.hook: You're trying to wean nike.com off promotions. That only works if the search experience is good enough to convert without a discount. Right now it isn't — and the CFO can see that in the numbers.
pain_points: Nike Digital revenue declined from ~$13.3B (FY2023) to ~$9.6B (FY2025) — $3.7B erosion in two years
CFO Matthew Friend Q3 FY2026: 'Digital is still too promotional. Markdowns across the marketplace remain elevated.'
Nike strategy is to reduce promotions and drive full-price sell-through — requires search relevance to convert
strategic_angles.discovery_question: The CFO said digital is 'still too promotional' — when you map search relevance to full-price sell-through, what is the current search conversion rate on nike.com, and what would a 1pp improvement mean to the digital revenue line?
strategic_angles.algolia_proof: At Nike's ~$9.6B digital baseline and 42.7% gross margin, 1% conversion lift via search = ~$96M revenue, ~$41M gross profit. Under Armour benchmark: +35% conversion on search sessions. [FACT — 04-competitors.md; 08-financial-profile.md]
strategic_angles.objection: We're in a cost-rationalization mode — not the time for new vendors.
strategic_angles.objection_counter: CFO Friend said in Q3 FY2026: 'We accelerated investments across Supply Chain and Technology.' Algolia replaces Elasticsearch maintenance cost while delivering better outcomes — it's ROI-positive, not additive spend.
strategic_angles.source: [FACT — 08-financial-profile.md (digital revenue trend, gross margin); 11-investor-intelligence.md (CFO quote 'still too promotional' Q3 FY2026)]

strategic_angles.label: The Composable Stack Signal: Nike Is Architecturally Ready for Algolia
strategic_angles.hook: Your architecture team is already building composable. Algolia is the search layer that plugs directly into your existing SFCC + Next.js stack — not a re-platforming project.
pain_points: Nike GRAND initiative: custom GraphQL/Next.js microservices on SFCC — composable, API-first architecture
Nike VP Marketing Data Linda Cereda confirmed evaluating 'composable stacks' and 'AI-native decisioning engines' — Martech Europe 2025
Algolia has purpose-built first-party SFCC AppExchange integration — no custom build required
strategic_angles.discovery_question: Your engineering team is building composable with GraphQL and Next.js. How does the team evaluate a best-of-breed search layer versus SFCC built-in Einstein search — especially given the new CIO's Salesforce background?
strategic_angles.algolia_proof: Algolia SFCC AppExchange integration — certified connector, native to the Nike stack. No custom integration project. [FACT — partner-intel.md; 02-tech-stack.md]
strategic_angles.objection: We'd need Cognizant to build the integration.
strategic_angles.objection_counter: Algolia has a purpose-built SFCC integration on the AppExchange. Nike's Next.js/GraphQL stack is a standard Algolia reference architecture. Integration is weeks, not a systems integration project.
strategic_angles.source: [FACT — 02-tech-stack.md (GRAND/GraphQL, SFCC, composable signal); 09b-social-signals.md (Signal 8 — composable stacks, Cereda Martech Europe 2025); partner-intel.md (SFCC co-sell, CIO background)]

strategic_angles.label: The Platform Mandate: Technology Now Under COO and CFO, Not a Siloed Tech Org
strategic_angles.hook: The CTO is gone. The CFO owns digital, and the COO owns technology. Both will approve search infrastructure spend when it's framed as a margin improvement, not a search upgrade.
pain_points: December 2025 'Win Now' restructure eliminated Nike's CTO role entirely
Technology now reports to COO Venky Alagirisamy — operational framing required
Nike Direct (DTC digital) now reports to CFO Matthew Friend — commercial outcome framing required
NDDC has three simultaneous Sr Director/Director vacancies — 60–90 day vendor evaluation window
strategic_angles.discovery_question: With the CTO role eliminated and technology now under the COO, how does a search platform evaluation get initiated at Nike — and who makes the final call: the CIO, COO, or CFO?
strategic_angles.algolia_proof: Algolia ROI maps to both buyer frames: COO (eliminate Elasticsearch maintenance, reduce Cognizant support tickets) + CFO (1pp search conversion = $41M gross profit on Nike Direct). [FACT — 08-financial-profile.md; partner-intel.md]
strategic_angles.objection: We're focused on finishing the Win Now restructure first.
strategic_angles.objection_counter: Finishing Win Now by May 2026 is exactly the window. Technology budget is actively being reallocated. A search platform decision inside the Win Now timeline gets FY2026 funding — after May it becomes a FY2027 procurement process.
strategic_angles.source: [FACT — 01-company-context.md (Dec 2025 restructure); 09d-hiring-signals.md (NDDC vacancies); 11-investor-intelligence.md (COO quote — 'fully integrated across the company')]

gap_pairs.said_quote: We are rewiring how we operate, and investing in the technology platforms that we expect will help us serve more consumers better.
gap_pairs.found_title: Nike search runs 100% proprietary — no AI search vendor, no personalization, score 4.3/10
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Network inspection confirmed api.nike.com/search. BuiltWith: no Algolia/Coveo/Bloomreach detected. Phase 2 audit: 3 HIGH severity gaps (NLP, Dynamic Facets, Recommendations). 9.27% outbound to Google.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia is the technology platform for consumer discovery. NeuralSearch + Personalization + Merchandising Studio = the platform investment Hill is making — with published ROI: Under Armour +35% conversion, Lacoste +150% search revenue.

gap_pairs.said_quote: Digital is still too promotional. Markdowns across the marketplace remain elevated.
gap_pairs.found_title: 9.27% of outbound traffic exits to google.com (+118% MoM) — search abandonment driving conversion failures
gap_pairs.found_evidence: SimilarWeb outgoing traffic (verified April 2026): google.com is #4 outbound destination at 9.27% share, up 117.81% MoM. Nike Digital declined from $13.3B to $9.6B in 2 years.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: When search surfaces the right product at the right price, customers buy without markdown. Algolia NeuralSearch converts high-intent queries that currently abandon to Google. Conservative ROI: $96M/year at 1% conversion lift.

positioning_matrix.prospect_with_algolia: NeuralSearch — understands intent, auto-applies price/attribute filters

positioning_matrix.prospect_today: Zero personalization detected. Browsing history ignored in search results.
positioning_matrix.prospect_with_algolia: Per-user re-ranking based on purchase history + browsing preferences

positioning_matrix.prospect_today: Static facets. “Cushioning Type” shown for apparel queries.
positioning_matrix.prospect_with_algolia: Context-aware filter panels that adapt to product type and query intent

positioning_matrix.prospect_today: Engineering-dependent. No banners on “sale.” No brand landing for “Jordan.”
positioning_matrix.prospect_with_algolia: Visual Merchandising Studio — no-code, instant campaign control

competitive_synthesis.scenario: GREENFIELD displacement — Nike runs DIY Elasticsearch while direct competitor Under Armour is already on Algolia
competitive_synthesis.scenario_note: Under Armour confirmed on Algolia with +35% search conversion; Nike runs same in-house Elasticsearch stack Under Armour replaced

icp_mapping.business_model: B2C (DTC via nike.com + retail stores) + B2B (wholesale)
icp_mapping.audit_type: GREENFIELD — no hosted search vendor; Elasticsearch DIY displacement
anchor_lines: Elliott Hill (Q3 FY2026): 'investing in the technology platforms that will help us serve more consumers better' — search infrastructure is the platform investment on the table.
Matthew Friend (Q3 FY2026): 'Digital is still too promotional' — Algolia converts high-intent queries at full price through relevance, not discounts.
Under Armour, Nike's direct competitor, is already on Algolia: +35% conversion rate on search. Nike runs the same DIY Elasticsearch stack Under Armour replaced in 2017.

priority_to_product.evidence: Matthew Friend, Q3 FY2026: 'Digital is still too promotional.'
priority_to_product.proof_result: +150% sales contribution from search; +37% conversion rate

priority_to_product.proof_result: +35% conversion rate when customers use search

Contact Cognizant retail account team — warm intro to Nike.com search engineering

touches.subject: Email 1 — Hook (Search Gap Finding)
touches.body:
# Email 1 — Hook (Search Gap Finding)

**Subject:** What I found when I searched "store near me" on nike.com

**Body:**

Hi [First Name],

Elliott Hill said on the Q3 earnings call that Nike is "investing in the technology platforms that will help us serve more consumers better." I spent 30 minutes on nike.com this week, and I found one thing that stood out immediately.

When a shopper types "store near me" into the nike.com search bar, they get 41 product results — t-shirts, sportswear — instead of a store locator. The intent is completely clear, and the site misses it entirely.

This is one of 10 gaps we documented across the search experience, all traceable to the custom Elasticsearch infrastructure running behind api.nike.com/search. The bigger ones — no personalization, NLP queries that partially parse intent but don't auto-apply price or size filters — connect directly to the "still too promotional" problem Matthew Friend described on the same call.

Worth a 15-minute conversation this week?

[Your name]
[Title], Algolia

---

**Source notes:**

- "store near me" returning product results: `09-browser-findings.md`, Step 2h, screenshot `08-non-product-store.png` [VERIFIED ON DISK]
- Elliott Hill quote "investing in the technology platforms": `11-investor-intelligence.md`, Q3 FY2026 earnings call transcript, Globe and Mail / Motley Fool WebFetch 2026-04-15 [FACT]
- Matthew Friend quote "digital is still too promotional": `11-investor-intelligence.md`, Q3 FY2026 earnings call transcript, Motley Fool WebFetch 2026-04-15 [FACT]
- Custom Elasticsearch at api.nike.com/search: `09-browser-findings.md`, Step 2a½ [FACT — network verification]
- 10 gaps enumerated: `09-browser-findings.md`, Summary section [FACT — browser audit 2026-04-15]

touches.subject: Email 2 — Competitor (Golden Angle)
touches.body:
# Email 2 — Competitor (Golden Angle)

**Subject:** Under Armour switched off their DIY search tool — here's what happened

**Body:**

Hi [First Name],

Under Armour was in the same position Nike is in today. They had built their own search infrastructure — in-house, open-source — and it worked, but it required engineering resources to maintain and it couldn't keep pace with how consumers shop.

They moved to Algolia. Their product manager described the result this way: "We moved from a manual, time-intensive search tool to a fast, intuitive and insight-driven search experience." The metric: 35% higher conversion rate when customers use search.

Nike.com is running a similar custom Elasticsearch stack — we confirmed it via network inspection on the api.nike.com/search endpoint. Under Armour's gain is a documented benchmark for what's possible on the same infrastructure trajectory.

I put together a short analysis of the specific gaps on nike.com — the ones that map most directly to what Under Armour solved. Happy to share it. Would a quick call this week make sense?

[Your name]
[Title], Algolia

---

**Source notes:**

- Under Armour on Algolia (Golden Angle — CONFIRMED): `04-competitors.md`, algolia.com/customers/under-armour WebFetch 2026-04-14 [FACT]
- "+35% higher conversion rate when using search": `04-competitors.md`, algolia.com/customers/under-armour [FACT — WebFetch verified]
- Nick Maupin quote: `04-competitors.md`, algolia.com/customers/under-armour [FACT]
- Nike custom Elasticsearch at api.nike.com/search: `09-browser-findings.md`, Step 2a½, network verification 2026-04-15 [FACT]
- Under Armour competitor classification: `04-competitors.md`, Competitor Search Technology Matrix, Row 3 [FACT]
- Golden Angle play used (not Defensive): BuiltWith confirmed via algolia.com/customers/under-armour case study page, not generic WebSearch

touches.body:
# LinkedIn — Follow-Up 1 (if accepted, no reply — Day 4-6)

## Version A — For Cheryan Jacob (CIO)

Hi Cheryan — the "store near me" gap is one of 10 we documented on nike.com. The bigger one: after browsing running shoes and then searching "shoes," the top results are Jordan retros. The site has no personalization layer — zero re-ranking based on browsing behavior. Nike's own app has NikeAI for this. The website doesn't. That's the infrastructure gap.

**Character count: 359** [UNDER 500 LIMIT — ACCEPTABLE]

---

## Version B — For Venkatesh Alagirisamy (COO)

Hi Venky — the personalization gap goes further than results ranking. Nike's site is running custom Elasticsearch at api.nike.com/search. The engineering overhead to maintain relevance tuning, merchandising rules, and facet logic is significant — and it still ships static facets (Cushioning Type shows up for jacket queries). Algolia replaces that maintenance burden with a managed platform. No fabrication — confirmed via network inspection.

**Character count: 430** [UNDER 500 LIMIT — ACCEPTABLE]

---

## Version C — For Matthew Friend (CFO)

Hi Matthew — one data point from the audit: 9.27% of outbound traffic from nike.com goes to Google, and that number grew 117.81% in one month. Those are shoppers who searched on Nike's site, didn't find what they needed, and left to search on Google instead. At 107.6M monthly visits, that's a material number of lost sessions. The mechanism is search relevance — and it's fixable.

**Character count: 404** [UNDER 500 LIMIT — ACCEPTABLE]

---

touches.subject: Email 3 — Business Case
touches.body:
# Email 3 — Business Case

**Subject:** One number from your FY2025 10-K that keeps coming up in our analysis

**Body:**

Hi [First Name],

Nike Brand Digital revenue is approximately $9.6 billion — derived from the FY2025 10-K (NIKE Direct at $18.8B, with the digital segment down 20% YoY). That's the baseline for the search revenue conversation.

Here's one component of the model: a 1% conversion lift on $9.6B digital = $96M/year incremental revenue. At Nike's 42.7% gross margin (FY2025 10-K), that's $41M in additional gross profit — from a single percentage point.

Matthew Friend said on the Q3 earnings call that Nike is "leveraging new advanced capabilities" in technology while rationalizing legacy programs. A managed search platform that replaces a self-maintained Elasticsearch stack fits both sides of that sentence — it's an advanced capability that eliminates maintenance overhead.

I have a full model built — six components, conservative and moderate scenarios. Happy to walk through it in 15 minutes. The leave-behind with the full analysis is also available here: [nike-leave-behind.html link]

[Your name]
[Title], Algolia

---

**Source notes:**

- NIKE Brand Digital ~$9.6B: `08-financial-profile.md`, Digital/Ecommerce Revenue table, derived from 10-K FY2025 [FACT — SEC EDGAR 10-K via last10k.com WebFetch 2026-04-14]
- NIKE Direct $18.8B, down 13%: `08-financial-profile.md`, Revenue Trend table; Nike FY2025 10-K MD&A [FACT]
- NIKE Brand Digital down 20% FY2025: `08-financial-profile.md`, 10-K MD&A verbatim [FACT]
- Gross margin 42.7% FY2025: `08-financial-profile.md`, Key Margins section, Gross Profit $19.790B ÷ Total Revenue $46.309B [FACT — Yahoo Finance MCP 2026-04-14]
- 1% conversion lift = $96M formula: `nike-playbook.md`, ROI Summary section [FACT — derived from above inputs]
- Friend quote "leverage new advanced capabilities": `11-investor-intelligence.md`, Q3 FY2026 earnings call, Globe and Mail / Motley Fool WebFetch 2026-04-15 [FACT]
- Leave-behind reference: `deliverables/nike-leave-behind.html` [VERIFIED ON DISK]

touches.subject: Loom Video Script — Nike Search Audit
touches.body:
# Loom Video Script — Nike Search Audit

**Target length:** 2 minutes (120 seconds)
**Word count:** 243 words
**Audience:** CIO / COO / CFO at Nike
**Recorded by:** AE

---

## HOOK (0–10 sec)

"I spent 30 minutes on nike.com and found three things that surprised me — given Nike's investment in digital. Let me show you exactly what I found."

*[Open screen share to browser with nike.com loaded]*

---

## DEMO (10–90 sec)

**Finding 1 — Screenshot: `08-non-product-store.png`** *(approx. 10–30 sec)*

"This is what happens when a shopper types 'store near me' into the nike.com search bar. They get 41 product results — t-shirts and sportswear — instead of a store locator. The intent is completely clear. The site misses it. This is a routing failure in the search infrastructure."

*[Transition: move to next screenshot]*

**Finding 2 — Screenshot: `17-personalization.png`** *(approx. 30–60 sec)*

"Here's the second thing. I browsed running shoes, clicked a product, then searched 'shoes.' The top results were Air Jordan retros — not running shoes. Zero personalization. No re-ranking based on what I just looked at. Nike's app has NikeAI for exactly this kind of intent detection. The website doesn't have that layer."

*[Transition: move to next screenshot]*

**Finding 3 — Screenshot: `14-dynamic-facets-drifit.png`** *(approx. 60–80 sec)*

"Third — I searched for a Dri-FIT UV running jacket. The facet panel showed 'Cushioning Type' as a filter. For a jacket. The facets are static — they don't adapt to what the shopper is actually looking for. This creates friction right at the moment of refinement."

*[Pull back to summary view]*

"Three gaps. Search misroutes intent, ignores browsing behavior, and serves irrelevant filters. Nike's digital channel is approximately $9.6 billion. These gaps compound across every session."

---

## CTA (90–120 sec)

"I put together a full analysis — 10 documented gaps, a business case with the revenue math, and a comparison to what Under Armour achieved after a similar infrastructure switch. Happy to walk you through it in 15 minutes.

[Your name], [Title], Algolia."

---

**Speaker notes — transitions:**

- After Finding 1: "That's the intent detection gap. Let me show you the personalization problem."
- After Finding 2: "That's the web experience versus the app experience. Here's the third gap — filtering."
- After Finding 3: "So three layers: routing, personalization, faceting. Here's the business context."

---

**Source notes:**

- Screenshot `08-non-product-store.png`: VERIFIED ON DISK (`deliverables/screenshots/08-non-product-store.png`) — "store near me" returning 41 product results, `09-browser-findings.md` Step 2h [FACT]
- Screenshot `17-personalization.png`: VERIFIED ON DISK (`deliverables/screenshots/17-personalization.png`) — Jordan retros shown after browsing running shoes, `09-browser-findings.md` Step 2q [FACT]
- Screenshot `14-dynamic-facets-drifit.png`: VERIFIED ON DISK (`deliverables/screenshots/14-dynamic-facets-drifit.png`) — Cushioning Type shown for jacket query, `09-browser-findings.md` Step 2n [FACT]
- $9.6B digital revenue: `08-financial-profile.md`, derived from Nike FY2025 10-K [FACT]
- Under Armour infrastructure comparison: `04-competitors.md`, Golden Angle section [FACT]
- All 3 screenshots confirmed present in `deliverables/screenshots/` [VERIFIED]

touches.subject: Email 4 — Social Proof
touches.body:
# Email 4 — Social Proof

**Subject:** Lacoste doubled their search sales contribution — without discounts

**Body:**

Hi [First Name],

Lacoste is a useful comparison for Nike right now. Global athletic/lifestyle brand, multi-territory ecommerce, similar pressure to drive full-price sell-through rather than leaning on markdowns.

Their gap was search relevance — shoppers were not finding the right product fast enough, so sessions were ending without conversion. Lacoste deployed Algolia across 11 online shops globally.

The result: search results delivered in under 50ms, +150% increase in sales contribution from search, +37% overall conversion rate on search (+62% on mobile), -88% bounce rate. Their Global UX Manager put it directly: "Not only does it provide the stellar user experience Lacoste is known for, but it more than doubled our global sales."

Matthew Friend has said Nike Digital is "still too promotional" — the mechanism behind that problem is that search relevance forces the discount crutch. When your system can't surface the right product at full price, markdown is the only conversion lever left. Lacoste's numbers show what happens when you remove that dependency.

Happy to connect this to the specific gaps we found on nike.com. Would a 15-minute call work this week?

[Your name]
[Title], Algolia

---

touches.subject: Email 5 — Break-Up
touches.body:
# Email 5 — Break-Up

**Subject:** Closing the loop on Nike

**Body:**

Hi [First Name],

I know you're deep in the turnaround work — the past few months have clearly been heads-down at Nike, and I don't want to add noise.

I'll leave you with the full audit we built on nike.com — 10 search gaps documented, a business case with the revenue math, and a comparison to what Under Armour achieved after making a similar infrastructure switch. It's all here if it's useful at any point: [nike-leave-behind.html link]

The door is open whenever the timing makes sense. Elliott Hill said he wants to move from "fixing this business" to "inspiring and driving growth" — when that chapter starts in earnest, the search infrastructure question will be on the table. We'll be here.

[Your name]
[Title], Algolia

---

**Source notes:**

- Elliott Hill "tired of fixing" quote: `11-investor-intelligence.md`, Nike All-Hands Meeting April 2026, Fortune WebFetch 2026-04-15 [FACT]
- Parting resource: `deliverables/nike-leave-behind.html` [VERIFIED ON DISK]
- Under Armour reference: `04-competitors.md`, Golden Angle section [FACT]
- 10 gaps documented: `09-browser-findings.md`, Summary / Key Gaps Found section [FACT — browser audit 2026-04-15]

Hi [First Name],

I know you're deep in the turn

contacts.name: Cheryan Jacob
contacts.title: Chief Information Officer
contacts.angle: Former Salesforce exec — SFCC co-sell warm entry. She knows Algolia from the AppExchange ecosystem. First call target.

contacts.name: Venkatesh (Venky) Alagirisamy
contacts.title: EVP & Chief Operating Officer
contacts.angle: Technology integration mandate. Frame search as operational capability — fewer Cognizant tickets, less Elasticsearch maintenance, faster feature velocity.

contacts.name: Matthew Friend
contacts.title: EVP & Chief Financial Officer
contacts.angle: Digital revenue recovery — CFO owns Nike Direct. Lead with ROI: $96M/year conservative at 1% lift. 'Digital is too promotional' is his pain.

abx_sequence.sequence_type: ABX Multi-Touch — Competitor Benchmark + Search Audit Reveal
abx_sequence.research_basis: nike.com audit findings, 09d-hiring-signals.md vacancy signals, partner-intel.md SFCC co-sell path, 11-investor-intelligence.md exec quotes

ae_fields.talk_track_opener: You know the SFCC ecosystem better than most — Algolia is the search layer purpose-built for it. Nike's Elasticsearch stack at api.nike.com/search is the foundation NikeAI runs on. We replace that with sub-20ms, AI-native search that integrates natively with SFCC, reduces Cognizant maintenance burden, and delivers the discovery experience that NikeAI proved works in the app — on the web.
ae_fields.talk_track_cta: 20-minute call to walk through the nike.com audit findings and the Under Armour benchmark (+35% search conversion, same DIY Elasticsearch Nike runs today).
ae_fields.next_step_action: Contact Algolia SFCC partner team — request joint introduction to Cheryan Jacob (CIO, former Salesforce exec). Lead with AppExchange co-sell framing, not cold outreach.
